1:
The chapter opens on a map, and the grammatical-historical commentators spend their care fixing the two armies upon it. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott agree that this Aphek — a name so common that Ellicott calls it a general word for a 'fort' or 'strong place' — must be carefully distinguished from the towns of the same name in Asher (Joshua 19:30; Judges 1:31), on the mountains of Judah (Joshua 15:53), and at Ebenezer (1 Samuel 4:1); this one lay in the plain of Jezreel, near Shunem. Adam Clarke sets it in the valley between Tabor and Gilboa and presses the strategic point that both hosts sought a copious spring, water being scarce in that country — the same instinct, he notes, that later drew Saladin and the Crusader Baldwin to rival fountains. On the Israelite spring the witnesses converge: Keil and Delitzsch identify it with Ain Jalud, 'Goliath's fountain,' issuing from the foot of Gilboa, and Ellicott corrects the Septuagint's mistaken 'En-dor,' which lay too far off to be Saul's camp.

John Gill works the same puzzle from the Reformed side, weighing whether the place is the Aphek of Judah or of Asher and settling on one near Jezreel and Shunem. But the commentators do not linger on topography for its own sake; the map is only the frame for a moral crisis.

That crisis is the Puritan Matthew Henry's whole concern. He sees David caught in 'a great strait,' and a greater one for a good man to see sin before him than to see trouble. Two roads lay open and both ran into guilt: to quit his post in the battle would brand him a coward and an ingrate to Achish his benefactor; to fight for the Philistines against Israel would make him a traitor to the people of God and forfeit forever his claim to the crown. Henry does not spare him — David 'brought himself into this strait by his own unadvisedness, in quitting the land of Judah, and going among the uncircumcised,' for it is strange if those who grow intimate with wicked people come off without guilt or grief or both. Yet because David's heart was upright, God 'would not suffer him to be tempted above what he was able, but with the temptation made a way for him to escape' (1 Corinthians 10:13). The witnesses thus read the encampment at Aphek not as the prelude to a battle David never fought, but as the stage God set to rescue his servant from a snare of his own making — the door of deliverance opening, as Henry says, through the very enmity of the Philistine princes.
2:
The single verse of the army's advance draws the grammatical-historical commentators to a study of Philistine order and of David's dangerous place within it. Charles Ellicott describes 'the orderly advance of this great military nation,' the five sovereign princes of the Philistine federation — Achish of Gath among them — marching with their chieftains; and he presses a distinction the English blurs: the 'lords' here are a different rank from the 'princes,' subordinate captains who nonetheless held great sway. Adam Clarke pictures the host divided as Israel's armies were, by fifties, hundreds, and thousands, each under its proper officer. Keil and Delitzsch fix the crucial detail of position: David and his men 'passed on in the rereward with Achish,' forming the rear-guard under the king's own eye, and they note that the objection to his presence was raised not at the setting out but later on the march, once the several princes had effected their junction.

The Reformed Gill guards the arithmetic — the 'lords' are not five men marching alone but each at the head of his hundreds and thousands — yet the weightier observation is Ellicott's, and it exposes David's whole predicament. In return for Ziklag, the town Achish had given him, David 'seems to have owed a kind of military service to his suzerain'; he marches now under the standard of Gath, a vassal in the Philistine line of battle. And he marches conspicuously: the arms and equipment of his Israelite band differed from the Philistine warriors around them, and 'the general appearance of the Hebrews was, of course, well known to their hereditary Philistine foes.' The very sight of David's men in the ranks was bound to raise the question the next verse records.

Here the witnesses do not debate; they build a single picture. David has followed one compromise to its edge — placed by his own choosing in the rear of a pagan army about to fall upon his own nation, honoured with the post of the king's bodyguard he had accepted at 1 Samuel 28:2. The rear-guard is a place of trust and a place of peril at once, and the reader, with the commentators, waits to see how God will extricate a servant so deeply entangled. The answer will come not from David's counsel but from the suspicion of the very lords whose ranks he has joined — the enmity that, all through this chapter, does him more good than any friendship could.
3:
The princes' blunt challenge — What do these Hebrews here? — and Achish's warm defence give the witnesses two things to examine: the standing of the men who object, and the curious phrase by which Achish measures David's time in his service. On the first, John Gill and Keil and Delitzsch agree that the objectors are not Achish's own courtiers but the confederate princes, the four other lords of the Philistine pentapolis, whose freedom to override the king of Gath betrays their rank. They are 'wroth' with him, Keil notes, and demand that David be sent back 'to his place,' lest in the battle he turn satan — an adversary — against them.

Against their suspicion Achish sets a generous testimony: this is David, who has been with him 'these days, or these years,' and in whom he has found no fault. The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on that odd doublet of time. Ellicott calls it 'an indefinite expression,' rendered variously by the versions, the Septuagint apparently taking it as 'two years'; he offers Maurer's smoother reading, 'who has been with me alway, for days, or rather for years.' Clarke agrees it marks no definite span — simply 'a considerable time.'

The Wesleyan John Wesley presses it into a small drama of speech: 'did I say days? I might have said years,' for David had dwelt a full year and four months in Philistia (1 Samuel 27:7) and had come to Achish once before (1 Samuel 21:10). Wesley even conjectures that after that first flight David 'might hold some correspondence with Achish,' judging him a man of more generous temper than the rest — which would account for the king's settled confidence. The phrase 'since he fell unto me,' the commentators note with Keil, means since David revolted or came over, the technical word for going over to another's side.

The witnesses do not so much divide here as accumulate a single irony. Achish's testimony is entirely sincere and entirely mistaken: he vouches for a fidelity David has taken pains to counterfeit. The princes, colder and shrewder, read the situation more truly than the king who loves him — and it is their distrust, not his trust, that God will use to save David from the field. As Henry observed of the whole episode, the enmity of the Philistine lords befriended David when no friend of his own could have done him such a kindness.
4:
The princes' anger hardens into a demand, and the witnesses read their reasoning as a lesson in worldly prudence that God bends to his own ends. Keil and Delitzsch render the core of it: send the man back to the place assigned him, that he not go down into the battle and become a satan, an adversary, in the ranks; for how better could David reconcile himself to his master Saul than 'with the heads of these men?' The fear is precise — that in the thick of the fight David would turn on the Philistine rear and buy his way back to Saul's favour with Philistine lives.

The grammatical-historical commentators add their notes. Charles Ellicott catches the technical force of 'go down,' the standing military word for the descent from the hill country to the plain where battle was joined. Adam Clarke observes a striking gap in the princes' knowledge: they had evidently not yet heard of David's raids from Ziklag upon the Geshurites and Amalekites (1 Samuel 27:8), for had they known, 'they would have seen much more cause for suspicion' — a deep irony, since that secret slaughter of desert tribes, undertaken to deceive Achish, would have damned David utterly in the princes' eyes. John Gill notes simply that their wrath fell on Achish for taking David's part.

The Wesleyan Wesley lifts the verse from policy to providence. The princes 'speak according to the rules of true policy,' for by such treachery great enemies have sometimes been reconciled; yet in their cold calculation 'the wise and gracious providence of God appeared.' God was using their suspicion to help David out of a strait 'out of which no human wit could have extricated him,' and, more, to send him home in time to recover all that the Amalekites were about to seize — a deliverance David had done nothing to deserve, since, Wesley adds, it had been most just for God to leave him in the distresses 'into which his own sinful counsel had brought him.'

The witnesses do not contend. They agree that the princes reasoned rightly by the light of statecraft, that they reasoned better than they knew, and that the hand which moved them was not their own. Human enmity, coldly self-interested, becomes in this verse the very instrument of a mercy David could never have engineered.
5:
To clinch their case the princes recall a song, and the witnesses read the moment for its irony and its history. The taunt they quote — Saul slew his thousands, and David his ten thousands — is the very refrain that first kindled Saul's jealousy, and Keil and Delitzsch trace it back to the women's celebration of David's victory over Goliath at 1 Samuel 18:7, adducing it, as they say, 'to justify their suspicion.' The princes' logic is sharp, for those 'ten thousands' were largely Philistine dead: a man so famed for the slaughter of their own people can hardly be trusted to fight their battles now, and least of all at their backs.

Charles Ellicott draws out what is remarkable in the quotation itself. These Hebrew 'folk-songs,' he observes, were 'current among the Philistines, who seem to have been a musical people' — the enemy had learned and preserved Israel's own war-chants. He connects it to a larger pattern: David himself, during his sojourn in Gath, appears to have 'learned and practised Philistine music,' which he afterward introduced into the worship at Jerusalem. The verse thus opens a small window on the cultural traffic between Israel and Philistia, even as it seals David's exclusion from the field; the same songs that passed between the two peoples now turn to David's disadvantage in the enemy's council.

The Reformed Gill adds only that the song was sung 'long ago,' underscoring how a triumph years past now rises to shape David's present. Here is the deeper irony the witnesses leave with us: the very renown that had made David the darling of Israel and the terror of Philistia — the honour he could never have wished away — becomes, in the mouths of the Philistine lords, the ground of his dismissal. As Matthew Henry mused of this same chapter, who would be fond of popular praise, when even that may another time be turned against a man to his reproach? The applause of the dancing women follows David into the enemy's camp, and there, by God's overruling, works for his rescue rather than his ruin. What was meant to shut him out of the battle proves the very means of keeping his hands clean of it, so that the taunt of the princes becomes, unknown to them, an instrument of mercy toward the man they distrust.
6:
Achish's dismissal of David is an oath, a testimonial, and a confession of his own weakness, and the witnesses take up each in turn. The startling thing, for the grammatical-historical readers, is the oath itself: 'as the LORD liveth' — by the life of Jehovah — on the lips of an idolatrous Philistine king. Charles Ellicott weighs it as 'a strange oath for an idolatrous prince,' and offers two explanations: either it is Achish's rough equivalent for his own gods, or, as Keil supposes, the king courteously swore by the God of David to make his sincerity fully felt by the man he believed unjustly suspected. Keil and Delitzsch translate the whole assurance — 'thou art honourable, and thy going out and coming in with me in the host is good in my sight; I have found no evil in thee unto this day; nevertheless the lords favour thee not.'

The Puritan Matthew Henry dwells on the graciousness of the discharge and the pathos of the king's position. Achish, 'though the chief, was but one of five,' and in the council of war 'was over-voted, and obliged to dismiss David, though he was extremely fond of him' — proof that 'kings cannot always do as they would.' Henry prizes the testimonial: 'thou hast been upright, and I have not found evil in thee' — a witness Saul, for all David's service, would never have given him; and he draws the rule that God's people should so behave 'as, if possible, to get the good word of all they have dealings with.'

Yet the Wesleyan Adam Clarke turns the compliment into an indictment. Achish thought David upright only because 'he had not yet heard of the transaction' — David had, in fact, 'given him to understand that he had been fighting against Israel' when he was raiding the desert tribes. The king's sincere praise rests upon David's studied deception, and the reader feels the sting the honest Achish cannot.

Around the oath a quiet debate gathers: does 'as the LORD liveth' betray some real leaning of Achish toward the God of Israel — Clarke will argue, at the next verse, that the king had perhaps learned much truth from David — or is it only the diplomatic courtesy Ellicott and Keil suppose? The witnesses leave it open. What they agree on is the moral shape of the scene: a pagan king speaks with transparent honesty and generous warmth, and the man of God stands silent under praise he has not earned, delivered from the battle by the distrust of enemies rather than the discernment of his friend.
7:
The king's plea is brief — return, and go in peace, that thou displease not the lords of the Philistines — and the witnesses treat it as the hinge on which David's whole deliverance turns. John Gill notes only the plain sense: David is sent back 'to Ziklag,' the town in Philistia that was his base, released from the march before a single blow is struck. Keil and Delitzsch read the words as Achish's regretful courtesy, the king still 'obliged to send David back' by the will of the four princes he cannot resist, softening the dismissal with the ancient blessing of peace and adding the anxious motive — 'that thou do nothing displeasing to the princes of the Philistines.' They observe too that David's own reply, just now to come, plays on the same ambiguity that has shielded him all along, for 'my lord the king' may be read of either Achish or Saul.

Yet the commentators hear in that formula, 'go in peace,' more than politeness. The peace Achish wishes is the very thing God is granting, though neither king nor captain yet sees its full extent. Matthew Henry, surveying the episode, calls this the door 'opened for his deliverance out of this strait' — for had David gone down into the battle, he must have sinned on the one hand or the other, betraying either Achish who trusted him or Israel whom he was bound to protect. Into this strait, Henry adds, David 'brought himself by his own unadvisedness,' and God might justly have left him there to chastise his folly; that the way of escape now opens is pure mercy. The command to withdraw closes both dangers at a single stroke.

The witnesses do not divide over so plain a verse; they let it stand as an emblem of providence working through unlikely hands. The lords' hostility, Achish's reluctant obedience to it, and the courteous word of dismissal all conspire to send David out of harm's way. He is spared the field not by his own contrivance but by the counsels of men who mean him no kindness, and even the enemy's farewell — go in peace — becomes, in the mouth of God's providence, a truer benediction than Achish knew. The peace he names is the peace of a conscience kept, against all likelihood, from a sin it had come perilously near committing.
8:
David's protest — But what have I done?... that I may not go and fight against the enemies of my lord the king? — is the ethical storm-centre of the chapter, and here the witnesses speak with rare unanimity of judgement, however they weigh the motive. John Gill states the plain fact: the question is 'anticipated' by Achish's own testimony, so David only feigns a concern he does not feel, putting on 'an appearance of concern for being dismissed, when he was heartily glad of it.' John Wesley is blunter still — 'this was deep dissimulation and flattery, no way to be justified' — and adds the sober warning that none knows how strong is the temptation to compliment and dissemble that besets those who attend great men.

The grammatical-historical readers press the same point from the text. Charles Ellicott hears in David's words 'a ring of falseness'; he 'never contemplated fighting in the ranks against Israel, and yet he speaks thus,' and the generous confidence of 'the chivalrous Achish is here in painful contrast with the dissimulation of the Israelite chieftain.' Adam Clarke agrees that whatever David secretly intended, 'he affects to feel his reputation injured' at not being trusted, having plainly let Achish believe he would fight against his own people.

Keil and Delitzsch supply the one escape valve in the grammar. David's phrase 'my lord the king' is deliberately ambiguous: Achish takes it of himself, but it might as truly mean Saul, so that David's words, false to the ear of the Philistine, could be inwardly reserved for the LORD's anointed. It is a defence of David's veracity by a hair's breadth — the sentence deceives without, strictly, lying.

The one real debate concerns design. Ellicott raises the possibility that David 'deliberately' provoked the princes' suspicion 'in order to bring about his dismissal from the army,' engineering his own rescue from an impossible spot; he grants it is only a suggestion. Most of the witnesses, with Gill and Wesley, read him more passively — a man caught, relieved, and covering his relief with courtly words. Either way the commentators refuse to whitewash him. Clarke draws the mercy plainly: it was 'in the order of God's gracious providence' that the lords refused him, for had he gone he faced 'a choice of two sins,' to fight against God and his country, or to betray the hospitable Achish. God spared him the deed by sparing him the battle, even while the words of this verse show how near the edge of sin his own dissembling had carried him.
9:
Achish crowns his praise with a simile — thou art good in my sight as an angel of God — and the witnesses divide gently over what a Philistine king could mean by it. Keil and Delitzsch give the plain sense of his renewed assurance: 'I know that thou art good in my sight, as an angel of God,' the strongest conviction of David's innocence the king can voice, before he yields to the princes and bids him depart at daybreak with 'thy master's servants.'

The interest gathers on the phrase itself. Adam Clarke takes it as evidence of Achish's religious leanings: the king 'speaks here of the angels of God as a Jew might be expected to speak,' and in the previous verse swore by Jehovah 'which, perhaps, no Philistine ever did'; Clarke thinks it 'possible that he might have learned many important truths from David during the time he sojourned with him,' even that he had come to favour the Jewish religion. John Wesley reads it more cautiously as borrowed language: the heathen 'acknowledged good spirits, which also they worshipped as an inferior sort of deities,' messengers to the supreme God, and Achish 'had learned the title of angels from the Israelites his neighbours, and especially from David's conversation' — the idea genuine, but fitted to a pagan's frame. Charles Ellicott takes the middle, deflationary view: it is 'a simile which Achish most likely borrowed from Hebrew thought, and made use of in his graceful courtesy as likely to be acceptable to David' — diplomacy more than devotion.

The Reformed Gill passes over the phrase to the hard clause that follows: 'notwithstanding the princes... have said, He shall not go up with us to the battle.' For all the king's affection, the decision is out of his hands.

So the debate is real but modest: does the angel-simile reveal a heart that David's sojourn had half-taught the truth (Clarke), or only a courteous tongue that had picked up Israel's idioms (Ellicott, Wesley)? The witnesses leave the king's soul in shadow. What stands clear is the pathos already noted — that the warmest tribute in the chapter, likening David to a messenger of God, falls from the mouth of the uncircumcised prince he has been deceiving, while the anointed of Israel accepts it in silence and prepares, with a relieved heart, to go.
10:
Achish's marching orders — rise up early with thy master's servants that are come with thee — set the witnesses a small riddle: who are these servants, and who is the master? The grammatical-historical commentators feel the difficulty most. Charles Ellicott confesses the phrase 'has perplexed expositors'; it is 'hardly the expression we should expect Achish to use of David's followers,' and 'all Israelites were, of course, subjects of Saul.' He floats 'a probable suggestion... lately made,' that the reference is to the men of Manasseh who had recently deserted to David (1 Chronicles 12:19) — warriors still known to some Philistines as Saul's soldiers, whose sudden appearance under David's standard may have first awakened the princes' suspicion.

Adam Clarke lays out the alternatives squarely. 'Thy master's servants' may mean David's six hundred Israelites, 'still the subjects of Saul though now residing in a foreign land'; or it may mean the servants of Achish, since on coming to Gath David had in effect given himself and his men to the king. But Clarke prefers a third and 'delicate' reading: that Saul is the master intended, and Achish is quietly informing David 'that he is no vassal, but still at liberty' — a courteous release from any bond of service to Gath.

The Reformed Gill takes the phrase at face value and finds in it the very sting the princes had felt: David's men are called 'the servants and subjects of Saul,' and this 'tacitly carried in it the objection of the Philistine lords' — that men who owed their true allegiance to Saul could not be trusted to fight against him, lest, 'finding an opportunity, they should seize it' to win back their king's favour. The words that dismiss David also name the ground of the suspicion that dismisses him.

The witnesses do not quarrel; they lay their readings side by side, and each illumines the scene. Whether 'master's servants' brands David's band as Saul's men (Gill), points to the newly-come Manassites who first drew Philistine eyes (Ellicott), or gently reminds David that he is a free man and no true vassal of Gath (Clarke), the phrase quietly confesses what the whole chapter has known: David and his six hundred belong, in the end, not to Achish but to Israel — and God is sending them home before the sword falls, at first light, and out of harm's way.
11:
The chapter ends as it began, with movement on the map, but now the witnesses read the departure as the seal of a double deliverance. John Gill catches David's eagerness: he and his men 'rose up early to depart in the morning,' as willing to be gone as the Philistines were to be rid of them. Keil and Delitzsch follow the two columns as they part — David turning back 'into the land of the Philistines,' that is, to Ziklag, while 'the Philistines went up to Jezreel' toward the battle that would cost Saul his life on Gilboa.

On David's heart in that hour Keil and Delitzsch, with Sebastian Schmidt, are eloquent: he returned 'no doubt very light of heart, and praising God for having so graciously rescued him out of the disastrous situation into which he had been brought, not altogether without some fault of his own' — rejoicing that he had committed no sin, having neither violated his pledged fidelity to Achish nor been forced to fight against Israel. The very ambiguity that had made his conduct perilous now leaves his conscience clear.

Charles Ellicott completes the picture with the irony the reader alone can see. David and his officers 'no doubt rejoiced at escaping the terrible alternative' of turning traitor to the kindly Achish or drawing sword against their own people; 'but they little thought in how sore a danger their wives and children and homes were at this juncture.' Their release from the Philistine army, Ellicott observes, 'was not a moment too soon to save these' — for while they marched with Achish, the Amalekites were falling upon Ziklag. The dismissal that felt like a disgrace was in truth a summons home to a rescue they did not yet know was needed.

The witnesses close in one accord. The enmity of the Philistine lords, the reluctant courtesy of Achish, and David's own relieved haste all served a providence larger than any of them. God delivered his servant from a sin his own compromise had courted, and in the same stroke sped him toward the deliverance of all he loved — the steps of a good man, as Henry says, ordered by the LORD even when he cannot see the way.
